---
title: "Chronos-2"
subtitle: "From zero-shot to agent-tuned foundation model forecasting"
---

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
metrics = FileAttachment("_data/metrics.json").json()
gapData = FileAttachment("_data/gap_data.json").json()
pipelineConfigs = FileAttachment("_data/pipeline_configs.json").json()
Plot = import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm")
```

## What is Chronos-2?

Chronos-2 is a **time series foundation model** by Amazon, pretrained on billions of time series observations from diverse domains. It produces probabilistic forecasts by tokenizing time series values and using a transformer architecture.

We use **Chronos-2** (120M parameters) via AutoGluon TimeSeries, which provides a clean interface for both zero-shot inference and LoRA fine-tuning. Unlike earlier Chronos-Bolt models, Chronos-2 natively integrates covariate information during inference.

### The pipeline the agent controls

```
Available data (at forecast origin)
    |
Covariate selection       <- agent chooses
    |
Data transformations      <- agent chooses (log_diff, pct_change, etc.)
    |
Context window            <- agent sets length
    |
Chronos-2 model (architecture FIXED, 120M params)
    |
Fine-tuning settings      <- agent chooses (on/off, LR, steps)
    |
Probabilistic forecast -> point forecast (mean)
```

## Zero-shot performance across countries

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof selectedCountry = Inputs.radio(
  ["norway", "canada", "sweden"],
  {label: "Country", value: "norway",
   format: v => ({"norway": "Norway", "canada": "Canada", "sweden": "Sweden"})[v]}
)

countryTargets = ({
  "norway": ["cpi", "industrial_production", "retail_sales", "unemployment"],
  "canada": ["cpi", "industrial_production", "retail_sales", "unemployment"],
  "sweden": ["cpi", "industrial_production", "unemployment"]
})
```

With no covariates and no fine-tuning, Chronos-2 relies purely on the shape of each target series:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-zs-vs-arima
//| fig-cap: "Zero-shot Chronos-2 vs ARIMA across target variables (MASE, validation era). MASE < 1 beats the random walk."

{
  const selected = metrics.filter(d =>
    d.country === selectedCountry && d.era === "validation"
    && ["arima", "zero_shot"].includes(d.method)
  );

  const targets = countryTargets[selectedCountry];
  const data = selected
    .filter(d => targets.includes(d.target) && d.mase != null)
    .map(d => ({
      method: d.display_name,
      variable: d.target.replace(/_/g, " ").replace(/\b\w/g, c => c.toUpperCase()),
      horizon: d.horizon,
      mase: d.mase
    }));

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 400,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 50,
    facet: {data, x: "variable"},
    x: {label: "Horizon", ticks: [1, 3, 6, 12]},
    y: {label: "MASE", grid: true},
    fx: {label: null},
    color: {
      domain: ["ARIMA", "Chronos-2 (zero-shot)"],
      range: ["#2ecc71", "#9b59b6"],
      legend: true
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([1.0], {stroke: "#999", strokeDasharray: "4,4"}),
      Plot.line(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "mase", stroke: "method",
        strokeWidth: 2, fx: "variable", curve: "monotone-x"
      }),
      Plot.dot(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "mase", fill: "method",
        r: 3, fx: "variable", tip: true
      })
    ]
  });
}
```

## The foundation model ladder

The three-step comparison: does zero-shot beat classical? Does agent-tuning improve over zero-shot? Does it survive out of sample?

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-ladder
//| fig-cap: "Average MASE: validation vs test for key configurations, per country."

{
  const data = gapData.map(d => ({
    country: d.country.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + d.country.slice(1),
    config: d.config,
    era: d.era === "validation" ? "Val" : "Test",
    mase: d.avg_mase
  }));

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 350,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 50,
    x: {label: null, padding: 0.3},
    y: {label: "Average MASE", grid: true, domain: [0.7, 1.15]},
    fx: {label: null},
    color: {
      domain: ["Val", "Test"],
      range: ["#3498db", "#e74c3c"],
      legend: true
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([1.0], {stroke: "#999", strokeDasharray: "6,4"}),
      Plot.barY(data, Plot.groupX({y: "identity"}, {
        x: "config", y: "mase", fill: "era", fx: "country",
        tip: true
      }))
    ]
  });
}
```

## Best discovered pipelines

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const rows = ["norway", "canada", "sweden"].map(c => {
    const cfg = pipelineConfigs[c];
    if (!cfg) return null;
    const bc = cfg.best_config;
    return {
      Country: c.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + c.slice(1),
      Covariates: bc.covariates?.join(", ") || "---",
      Context: bc.context_length || "all",
      "Fine-tune": bc.fine_tune ? `Yes (${bc.fine_tune_steps})` : "No",
      "Val MASE": cfg.best_score.toFixed(3)
    };
  }).filter(Boolean);

  return Inputs.table(rows, {
    columns: ["Country", "Covariates", "Context", "Fine-tune", "Val MASE"],
    width: {Country: 80, Covariates: 250, Context: 60}
  });
}
```

**Key insight:** Norway and Canada rely on fine-tuning (500 steps). Sweden achieves strong results through covariate selection and context truncation alone --- and its gains generalize better to the test era.
